text: The cautery scissor in the right hand, the monopolar, fenestrated monopolar, fenestrated Maryland bipolar in the left hand, and then the ProGrasp in the fourth arm. And then immediately you can see there the ROSI catheter. The ROSI catheter is a robotic operated suction irrigation catheter, and it allows me as the surgeon to do my own suctioning, which we really appreciate. And you'll see this later in the procedure, and I'll comment on it. But basically what we have done so far is we have the patient in Trendelenburg, And we have the camera port at the umbilicus. And then we have the scissor port is in the right lower quadrant, but pretty much at the umbilical level. We have one single eight millimeter air seal assistant port that we place between the scissor arm there on the right side and the camera port. And that eight millimeter port is what gives us our air seal insufflation and the assistant will use that to hand me needles and to put the bag for the specimen and things like that. They won't be using that for suction because I'll be doing my own suctioning. So the assistant doesn't really have to do very much when I do most of my robotic operations because I do my own suctioning. And so that adds some consistency to the operation so that I don't have to rely on having really an expert bedside assistant. I can pretty much do it with anyone as long as they know how to run the bag and they know how to change the instruments. They know how to get needles in and out. I do my own clipping with the robotic clip appliers you'll see throughout this procedure as well. So again, just reduces the reliance on the assistant and particularly for those surgeons who have some inconsistency when it comes to who's bedside assisting them, if they have different people every time or they have residents sometimes, maybe interns, this is a way that you can kind of become more independent. So I think what you'll find watching this prostatectomy procedure is that the surgeon really has a lot of control over the operation and is able to kind of keep things flowing and really be able to do all of the critical portion of the operation, including the clipping, obviously, which is very important.

The surgeon releases the bowel to prevent injury during instrument changes and to avoid inadvertent burning. The initial incision is made approximately one centimeter above the line between the white peritoneal reflection on the bladder and the yellow perirectal fat. The seminal vesicles and vas deferens are exposed through a posterior dissection, with the vas deferens visible in the midline and the seminal vesicles positioned closer to the rectum.

The surgeon lifts the vas deferens with the robotic fourth arm, revealing the seminal vesicle positioned closer to the rectum. The medial dissection is performed first, followed by repositioning the fourth arm to visualize the lateral aspect of the seminal vesicle. The surgeon carefully retracts the seminal vesicle, ensuring it is not in a hole, and uses bipolar cautery to dissect tissue directly on the seminal vesicle, avoiding nerve damage by staying close to the structure.

The surgeon uses cautery set at twenty-five to dissect around the seminal vesicles, carefully hugging the lateral aspect to avoid nerve injury. The vas is exposed from the posterior side, providing a clear view of the seminal vesicles.

The surgeon uses the fourth robotic arm to lift the vas deferens straight up, providing counter traction to stabilize the seminal vesicle for dissection. The medial aspect of the seminal vesicle is freed up, and the dissection proceeds towards the tip. The fourth arm is adjusted to show the lateral aspect by moving into the contralateral shoulder position. The surgeon carefully hugs the seminal vesicle, peeling vessels off its surface to avoid nerve injury, using bipolar cautery set at twenty-five to minimize thermal spread.

The surgeon positions the fourth arm to provide counter traction, exposing the lateral aspect of the seminal vesicle. They carefully dissect along the lateral border, staying close to the seminal vesicle to avoid nerve injury. The surgeon uses bipolar cautery to buzz and peel vessels off the seminal vesicle, ensuring not to veer off and cause thermal injury to the nerves located laterally. The fourth arm is adjusted frequently to maintain tension and prevent working in a confined space. The vas deferens is visible, and the surgeon plans to cut it later in the procedure.

The surgeon carefully dissects the seminal vesicle (SV), cutting the vas deferens at the edge of the SV to prevent it from obstructing the dissection. The SV is mobilized towards the prostate, ensuring the tip is free for easier manipulation later. The posterior leaflet of Denonvilliers' fascia is visible, and the incision is made above the yellow perirectal fat to avoid rectal injury. The bladder is dropped to enter the space of Retzius, using a technique that involves incisions to the left and right of the ligaments before addressing the midline.

The surgeon makes an incision to the right of the ligament, revealing the pubic bone through blunt dissection. A natural plane is identified, characterized by a cobweb-like appearance, separating perivascular fat from the lateral sidewall and nodes. The surgeon avoids cutting through excessive fat, maintaining the correct plane for node dissection.

The surgeon makes incisions on both sides to locate the pubic bone, ensuring the dissection remains in the correct plane. The cobweb-like tissue is dissected bluntly, with cautery used to address small bleeders. The bladder is dropped, exposing the node plane and separating perivascular fat from the sidewall to facilitate node dissection.

The surgeon uses a combination of blunt and sharp dissection to address an adherent area, revealing prominent lymph nodes on the left side. Fat is reduced from the obturator canal to improve mobility near the prostate base. The superficial venous complex is exposed, and excess fat on the prostate is removed to clear the view for bladder neck dissection. The cautery setting is adjusted to 40-40 for node dissection, ensuring minimal bleeding.

The surgeon uses the fourth arm for retraction, incising the endopelvic fascia to better identify the bladder neck for dissection. The surrounding fat is cleaned off, and the obturator nerve is visible in the defined plane between the perivascular fat and nodes. The surgeon prepares to perform the node dissection before proceeding with the prostatectomy.

The surgeon makes an incision over the vein, carefully dissecting tissue directly on top of it to better identify anatomical planes. Bipolar energy is used extensively to seal lymphatics and minimize bleeding, avoiding monopolar cautery to prevent vein injury and obturator nerve stimulation. Clips are placed distal to the node of Cloquet and proximal near the obturator nerve to prevent back bleeding of lymphatic fluid.

The surgeon uses bipolar energy to seal lymphatics and places a clip on the lymphatics distal to the node of Cloquet to minimize lymphatic leak from the leg. Another clip is placed on the proximal end of the lymphatic packet. The surgeon carefully seals lymphatics around the packet to prevent lymphoceles.

The surgeon performs an anatomic dissection of the lymph node packet, using bipolar energy to seal lymphatics around the pelvic sidewall and vein. The dissection follows the anatomical structures, including the obturator nerve and pelvic sidewall muscle, ensuring complete removal of nodes from the nodal basin. The procedure involves a limited node dissection targeting iliac and obturator nodes, avoiding extension towards the common iliacs due to the patient's intermediate risk profile.

The surgeon places a second clip to prevent backflow of lymphatics from the node packet being removed, ensuring both the proximal and distal ends are clipped. Bipolar cautery is used to seal lymphatics between the clips, minimizing the risk of lymphocele formation. The cautery is set at forty to manage thermal spread and avoid complications such as vein injury or obturator nerve stimulation. The surgeon dissects deeper nodes beyond the obturator nerve, revealing internal iliac vessels on the medial side, while retracting the bladder medially to clear fat from the surgical field.

The surgeon uses a rosy catheter to suction fatty fluid, improving visibility and contact with the cautery during the node dissection. The dissection is performed meticulously on the underside of the vein, ensuring no lymphatics are left behind. Clips are applied to prevent lymphatic leaks, and the surgeon prepares to repeat the procedure on the opposite side before proceeding to the prostatectomy.

The surgeon uses the fourth robotic arm to push the bladder to the right side of the screen, exposing the surgical field. They begin by identifying the vein and proceed to remove overlying fat tissue to prevent obstruction during the procedure. The surgeon carefully dissects around the vein, ensuring to peel off surrounding tissue while maintaining anatomic dissection. Visible lymphatics are identified and sealed to prevent leakage. The epigastric vessels are noted as they branch from the iliacs, and care is taken not to injure these vessels during the dissection.

The surgeon has defined the anterior surface of the vein and is preparing to place a clip at the node of Cloquet using a robotic clip applier. The clip is positioned precisely with the wristed instrument to ensure accurate placement, minimizing the risk of nerve injury or leaving prostate tissue behind. The dissection extends to the pelvic sidewall, removing fat from the underside of the vein to maintain a clean anatomical dissection.

The surgeon uses scissors to retract the vein without causing damage, while employing bipolar cautery to minimize current through the obturator nerve. The obturator artery is visible, descending into the obturator canal, and may be sacrificed if necessary. The surgeon places a clip on the proximal end of the node packet, ensuring not to clip on top of a node to avoid leaving part behind, which could risk a lymphocele. The obturator nerve is carefully defined to prevent accidental clipping, which could lead to temporary leg adduction palsy.

The surgeon carefully thins out the tissue at the corner where the clip will be placed, ensuring all lymphatics are included without clipping the nerve. The nerve is identified and separated from surrounding tissue, and deeper dissection is performed to access internal nodes potentially involved by prostate cancer. The surgical field becomes fluid-filled, requiring suction to improve visibility and effectiveness of the bipolar cautery.

The surgeon performs a dissection of the internal iliac nodes, revealing the internal iliac artery. There is a small amount of bleeding in the corner, which is managed using suction. The surgeon uses bipolar cautery to control bleeding, noting the absence of a clip placement in this instance.

The surgeon uses a robotic suction device to manage bleeding in hard-to-reach corners during the node dissection. The anterior prostate fat is bagged and set aside. The scope is cleaned for better visibility before proceeding to the bladder neck dissection.

The surgeon reduces the cautery setting to 25 to minimize charring while dissecting the bladder neck. The tissue appears mostly white, with visible longitudinal muscle fibers, indicating proximity to the prostate. The incision is made over the prostate, with backward pressure applied to separate the bladder from the anterior prostate. The surgeon uses the prostate's contour to guide the dissection, ensuring the correct path to the urethra. The fourth arm is used to lift the urethra towards the anterior abdominal wall, aiding in identifying the posterior bladder neck. The surgeon starts the incision at the corners to maintain a tight bladder neck, avoiding a large opening.

The surgeon carefully dissects the bladder neck, focusing on releasing the corners first to maintain a tight 'O' shape. The left hand provides stretch while the monopolar cautery is used to split the longitudinal muscle fibers with minimal char. The posterior bladder neck is released, ensuring the bladder neck remains tight. The seminal vesicles, dissected earlier, are visible, aiding in confirming the correct dissection path.

The surgeon completes the bladder neck dissection, revealing the vas deferens. The dissection proceeds on the lateral aspects of the prostate, removing bladder muscle fibers down to the perivascular fat, exposing vessels. The bladder is fully released and not obstructing the view of the rectum. The rectal dissection is performed by entering the perirectal fat plane, leaving a leaflet of Denonvilliers fascia on the posterior prostate, which provides a margin barrier for potential cancer on the posterior side.

The surgeon performs a high anterior release of the prostatic fascia to expose the neurovascular bundle, ensuring the incision is made high to avoid injuring nerves responsible for erections. The robotic clip applier is used to secure the pedicle close to the prostate, preventing damage to the neurovascular bundle as it turns posterolateral to the gland.

The surgeon performs blunt dissection on the posterolateral aspect of the prostate gland, releasing the neurovascular bundle by making a small incision in the lateral edge of the Denonvilliers fascia. The air seal system maintains pneumo pressure at six millimeters of mercury, minimizing venous bleeding while the Rosie suction catheter continuously removes smoke and fluids from the surgical field.

The surgeon places a clip at the base of the prostate, ensuring it is close to the prostate capsule to maximize nerve sparing. The clip is applied to the pedicle in one motion, maintaining the integrity of the neurovascular bundle. The dissection continues with blunt techniques to peel the neurovascular bundle off the prostate, requiring occasional snipping of the Denonvilliers' fascia. The nerve-sparing dissection extends to the apex of the prostate, ensuring the neurovascular bundle is preserved.

The surgeon cuts through the dorsal venous complex (DVC) without pre-placing a stitch or using a stapler, opting to over-sew it later. This approach allows flexibility in cutting the DVC closer to the prostate in low-risk cases, minimizing the risk of injuring the neurovascular bundle. Despite visible venous bleeding, the pneumo pressure of six effectively controls it. The urethra is exposed, and the catheter is pulled back to reveal the posterior aspect before cutting through it, marking the completion of the prostatectomy.

The surgeon places a figure-of-eight stitch using an ovicryl suture with a CT-one needle to secure the dorsal venous complex (DVC), staying medial to the neurovascular bundle. The stitch is placed horizontally above the level of the neurovascular bundle, minimizing the risk of injury. Despite some venous bleeding, the visibility remains adequate for precise suturing.

The surgeon places additional sutures to incorporate the remaining dorsal venous complex (DVC) into the stitch, ensuring hemostasis. The puboprostatic ligaments are also included in the stitch. The DVC stitch is then anchored through the pubic symphysis to compress the DVC against the bone, preventing postoperative bleeding. The surgeon prepares a CT-1 ovicryl suture for the Rocco stitch, used for posterior reconstruction. The Rosy suction catheter is employed to clear blood clots for better visibility during the procedure.

The surgeon incorporates the edge of the peritoneum into the stitch to close the posterior opening, preventing any tissue from moving behind the bladder. A single slider knot is used to align the bladder and urethra, reducing tension on the anastomosis and controlling potential bleeding from small arteries and venous sinuses. The Stratafix barbed suture is employed for the anastomosis, starting at the four or five o'clock position, with two stitches through the urethra and three through the bladder, ensuring a secure posterior wall connection.

The surgeon uses their left hand to identify the urethral edge and places a suture through the mucosa without the aid of a catheter. The stitch is taken up to the ten or eleven o'clock position, ensuring mucosal apposition to prevent leaks. The suction catheter is used to clear the field before tightening the suture, emphasizing the importance of mucosa-to-mucosa contact for a watertight anastomosis.

We used to take the catheter out as early as day three. So we would take the catheter out day three, four, five, six, or seven, depending on scheduling for the clinic. And what we found was, and we published this in the Journal of Urology years ago, is if we took the catheter out too early, the risk of retention was eleven times higher. So we stopped taking the catheter out on day three or four because the risk of retention was eleven times higher. I think it's because there's just still too much edema at the anastomosis. So even though they're not going to have a urine leak, and we demonstrated that on cystograms, we used to do cystograms on everyone, we do them very selectively now, rarely, But we used to do cystograms on everyone. And even at three days, the risk of urine leak was less than one percent. But even though the risk of urine leak at day three or four is less than one percent, the risk of retention was higher than we'd like. So that's why we found we have to leave the catheter in at least five days. So we do five to seven days. And it's worth it for the patients because if they have to have a catheter back in, that's more miserable than just wearing the catheter for an extra day or two. So now we have finished our anastomosis as you can see. So the catheter, the new catheter, we use a new Foley catheter now. It's an eighteen French that the assistant has put in. They are filling the bladder with saline just to test our anastomosis. We obviously don't see any urine leak. So we'll take our needles out and then we'll bag our prostate and our procedure is done. So this patient now will go to an observation unit postoperatively. They'll get them up walking, give them something to eat. And then, again, most of the time they'll go home the same day. And we really offer it to all patients. We offer it to everyone, and we leave it up to the patient to decide. But again, particularly in the era of using the ultra-low pneumo of six, which this entire procedure was done in a pneumo of six, other than when we put the ports in at the beginning, we turn the pneumo up for that. But once we docked the robot, we turned it down to six. The whole case was done at six. And so these patients have less pain. Particularly the two major things that we've seen that have completely been eliminated is the scrotal swelling that they get afterwards, as well as the shoulder pain. We used to think the shoulder pain was because of just the gas that's left behind. But actually, we don't see shoulder pain anymore. And I believe that's because we're not doing that peritoneal stretch and pressure on the diaphragm. So we have no scrotal swelling, we have no shoulder pain. So that's our robotic prostatectomy with lymph node dissection. Again, want to thank our sponsors for providing this opportunity to develop this educational video. I hope that this has been educational. You may want to watch it more than once to pick up on any of the little tips or tricks that we pointed out. And I look forward to meeting you at some point in the future. If I ever see you at a conference, please say hello. And also I would encourage you to come out and visit us. We have visiting surgeon programs here at our hospital in our suburb of Columbus, Ohio. And so you're welcome to come out and visit us to observe surgeries, whether it be prostatectomies or kidney surgery or anything else. And again, hopefully this has been beneficial to you, and more importantly, beneficial to your patients. So thank you once again, and please feel free to share this with any colleagues. And if ever any questions or even criticisms, please look for me at conferences. Thanks.
